text: The surgeon performs a single incisional laparoscopic total colectomy, creating a left-sided ileostomy. The procedure is adapted due to the patient's high steroid use and medically refractory ulcerative colitis.
The patient is positioned in the modified lithotomy position, with legs elevated and spread apart, allowing optimal access to the surgical site for the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon excises a disc-shaped section of previously marked skin in the left lower quadrant and enters the abdomen using a muscle-splitting technique.
The surgeon introduces one 12mm and two 5mm trocars through the jet platform, establishing access points for laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon begins dissection from the right colon, identifying and ligating the ileocolic pedicle.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection of the colon, progressing through the tissue layers with visible separation of anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and carefully preserves it during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the ascending colon and performs a total colectomy, moving in a clockwise direction from right to left.
The surgeon takes down the hepatocolic ligament and mobilizes the transverse colon by dividing the mesocolon and the greater omentum.
The surgeon ligates the left branch of the middle colic artery after taking down the splenic flexure.
The surgeon staples the recto-sigmoid junction while leaving the inferior mesenteric artery intact, completing the dissection and preparation for resection.
The surgeon exteriorizes the colon through the ileostomy site, completing the mobilization and removal process.
The surgeon matures the ileostomy, leaving the rectal stem in the pelvis without implantation.

The surgeon performs a circumferential incision around the stoma site in the left lower quadrant, releasing the loop ileostomy. The ileostomy is temporarily sealed with a trio-viral stick and returned to the abdomen.
The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum using a single port access device, inflating the abdominal cavity to facilitate the surgical procedure.
The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery at the level of the superior artery, navigating through the avascular plane posterior to the mesorectum.
The surgeon performs dissection down to the levator muscles, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues dissection on both sides upon reaching the levator muscles, followed by anterior mobilization of the tissue.
The surgeon performs a digital rectal exam to confirm the level of dissection after achieving complete mobilization of the rectum.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic GIA-XVI stapler to apply two staple fires, securing the tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a 15 to 20 centimeter ileal pouch using the ILA stapler, forming the pouch by sequentially firing the stapler to shape the ileal tissue.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the bowel, which shows no leakage before proceeding with the anastomosis.
The surgeon places a persisting stick with 2-0 Prolene suture and inserts an EEA 28mm stapler into the ileal pouch.
Under laparoscopic view, the surgeon creates an intracorporeal anastomosis and performs a pouchoscopy to inspect the newly formed ileal pouch.
A loop of bowel proximal to the ileal pouch is identified and exteriorized, followed by the maturation of the ileostomy.
